findings.description: yaml.load() is called without a SafeLoader, allowing arbitrary Python object construction and code execution from an untrusted config file.
Precedence is inverted versus the documented 'env > file > defaults': env is merged before the file, so file values override environment variables.
Malformed YAML raises yaml.YAMLError which is uncaught and propagates out of _read_file/load instead of being handled or logged.
feature_enabled assumes feature_flags is a dict, but file or env can set it to a non-mapping (e.g. a string), causing AttributeError on .get.
get() re-runs full load() (file read + env scan) on every call, so there is no caching and repeated accesses re-parse the YAML file each time.
require() calls get() which itself calls load(), compounding the per-access full reload cost.
Env overlay lowercases key names and cannot express nested keys, so APP_ overrides only match flat lowercase keys and silently miss nested/differently-cased config.
require() conflates a missing key with a key explicitly set to None, raising KeyError for legitimately null-valued configuration.
_coerce accepts Python int/float quirks (underscores like '1_000', surrounding whitespace, 'inf'/'nan') producing surprising typed values from raw env strings.
open() has no error handling for permission/IO errors (e.g. unreadable file), so OSError propagates despite the missing-file case being handled gracefully.
get() returns the fallback only when the key is absent; a key whose stored value is None returns None rather than the provided fallback.
A feature flag whose value is a truthy non-bool (e.g. the string 'false') is coerced to True by bool(), which may not match operator intent.
Optional is imported and used in annotations but Optional[Any] is redundant since Any already includes None.
_read_file and _read_env lack docstrings while sibling public methods are documented, giving inconsistent documentation coverage.
